other_observers: none; I was the original finder and identifier.
specific_location: Fountain Creek Regional Park. It was on the northern edge of North Rice's pond just on the western edge.
description: The bird initially was facing away from me and gave me good looks at the back side which showed the streaking on the back (of course this does not rule out everything else). It was somewhat grayish/tan in color on the back with "blackish" streaks. The tail appeared a yellow-green color. The fringes of the wings were white with the base wing color seeming to be black in color.  The bird then moved in the tree and gave me more of a frontal view, showing the small black throat area and black at the base of the bill just into the eyes. The bill had the size and shape of a Bullock's Oriole. There was a yellow-orange coloring on the head and breast down towards the belly but it did seem to fade out near the base of the belly. I did not note the undertail coverts color. The yellow-orange color was much more "orangy" around the immediate face area. Becoming almost a orange-red color in the "cheek" area or just around the black throat patch.  The size of the bird was very similar to a "Northern" Oriole. But dimensions seemed different with the tail seeming longer. See similar species sections for the tail length description. The overall coloring of the bird was that of a Streak-backed Oriole and not that of a "Northern" Oriole. Not sure how to explain that but if you are familiar with Streak-backed birds in this plumage you know that the coloring just is not that of a "Northern" or any other species of Oriole that i have seen. Young Altamira Orioles might look similar in color but see similar species section for that.   I am speculating here on the age and sex of the bird, 1st year male. If I got the sex correct then I think I got the age correct. If the bird was a female then I assume it was an adult as I don't believe a 1st year female would show the black throat and even if it did I don't think it would show quite as bright orange around the face. The other reason I think it was a first year bird is how the color on the belly diffused to almost gray and I think an adult female would have been bright all the way down. But I am only speculating.Behaviors: No real behaviors in supporting the ID other than general jizz of the bird being that of an oriole. The bird caught my attention as I was talking on the phone it flew up from either the ground, low bush or possibly the sewage "pond." It sat in a leafless elm at first looking away from me and bent over. It sat up and then "flitted" up just a bit in the tree. and faced me and then moved again down a bit and gave more of a profile look before finally flying almost straight over me into some thickets. Not to be seen again.Call: n/aPlumage: unknown
